summary: Asparouhova observes that women in her peer group tend to pursue graduate school while men gravitate toward tech and entrepreneurship. She draws a parallel to Freakonomics' analysis of baby naming trends, suggesting that prestigious career paths may follow similar patterns—becoming attractive to men first, then 'trickling down' to women just as they lose their cachet. She notes that programming was once considered women's work until it gained status, at which point women were pushed out. The provocative conclusion: women may never have been collectively represented in positions considered powerful by their contemporaries, and true career strategy means staying ahead of trends rather than following them.
key_points: 58% of first-time graduate students are women, while men increasingly pursue tech and entrepreneurship
Career prestige may follow Freakonomics-style patterns: high-status groups adopt paths first, then abandon them as others follow
Programming was considered 'women's work' in the 1960s until it gained status and prestige, then women were pushed out
Women may succeed in becoming tech founders in 30 years, but only after the role loses its current prestige
The strategic career move is to anticipate the next prestigious path rather than following current trends
money_quotes: Maybe graduate school was the thing to do until recently, but now men have turned their eye toward pursuing careers in tech and entrepreneurship, leaving women behind.

Perhaps it's not that things were once better and then got worse. It's that women have simply never been collectively represented in a position considered to be respected and powerful by their contemporaries.

30 years from now, I can confidently predict there will be plenty of women who are tech founders, even if we cease all gender equality efforts right now. But did that mean we succeeded, if being a tech founder is no longer en vogue?
Rather than focus on getting a graduate degree, learning to code, or starting a company, the goal should be to stay ahead of the trends.
key_insight: Women may consistently achieve representation in prestigious career paths only after those paths have lost their status, suggesting the real barrier isn't access but the moving target of what society values.
